headline: Lewis and Clark Reach Marias River Confluence, 1805
body:
The second day of June, in the year of our Lord eighteen hundred and five, dawned upon the Corps of Discovery with a fair countenance, though the preceding night had been rent by violent winds and a fleeting shower. With the sun’s first golden kiss upon the eastern horizon, the expedition stirred, eager to press onward into the vast, untamed heart of the continent. The Missouri, that grand, serpentine artery of the West, flowed with a strong, yet regular, current, its banks offering firm purchase for the towlines that pulled the canoes against the ceaseless flow. The river bluffs, which had for so long dominated the landscape, now began to recede, yielding to plains more level and expansive, where the timber, though still sparse, hinted at a richer abundance ahead. It was a land teeming with life, a vibrant tapestry woven by the hand of the Creator. Game, in its wild profusion, became a pressing concern, for the leather boat, that ingenious contrivance, would soon demand a covering of elk skins. Thus, Captain Lewis, with a keen eye for both necessity and the pulse of the wilderness, joined the hunters ashore. The day’s bounty was rich: six elk, two buffalo, two mule deer, and a formidable bear, all in prime condition, their flesh a welcome addition to the expedition’s larder. Yet, the hunt was not without its perils, for the bear, a creature of immense power and ferocity, nearly claimed the intrepid Drewyer, and pursued Charbono, who, in a moment of desperate ingenuity, fired his gun into the air as he fled, finding refuge in the dense thicket until Drewyer, with a single, well-aimed shot to the head, brought the magnificent beast down. Such encounters, raw and elemental, underscored the untamed grandeur of this new world. As the day waned, the expedition made camp on the larboard side, nestled in a handsome bottom of small cottonwood, directly opposite the mouth of a river of considerable size. The hour was late, too late for a thorough examination, but the promise of the morrow, and the favorable evening for celestial observations, held the company in hopeful anticipation.

The dawn of June third brought with it the weight of a momentous decision. The camp was struck upon the very point where the two great rivers converged, a geographical riddle that demanded immediate and precise resolution. Which of these, the left or the right, was the true Missouri, the fabled waterway that the Minnetaree people called “Amahte Arz zha,” the river that, by their accounts, drew nigh to the distant Columbia? The gravity of the choice pressed upon the captains, for to err now, with two months of the traveling season already spent, would be to squander precious time, perhaps even to unravel the very fabric of the expedition. To ascend the wrong stream, only to discover its true course far within the embrace of the Rocky Mountains, and then be forced to retrace their steps, would not only consume the season but might, in its crushing disappointment, dishearten the entire party, threatening the expedition’s ultimate success. Thus, with a profound sense of responsibility, Captain Lewis penned in his journal, “convinced we were that the utmost circumspection and caution was necessary in deciding on the stream to be taken.” To this end, a meticulous investigation was launched. Light canoes, each bearing three men, were dispatched up both rivers, while small parties ventured overland, tasked with penetrating the country as far as prudence allowed, their mission to discern the distant bearings of these enigmatic waterways from the vantage of the rising grounds. From a lofty height in the fork of these rivers, Captain Clark and Lewis surveyed an expansive and enchanting panorama: a vast plain, stretching in every direction, alive with innumerable herds of buffalo, attended by their wolfen shepherds, and the solitary antelope, now with their young, scattered across the verdant expanse. Elk grazed peacefully, and the ground itself was cloaked in a rich, green mantle. To the south, a range of lofty mountains, snow-capped and majestic, stretched from southeast to northwest, a continuation, they surmised, of the “S. Mountains.” Beyond these, a second, even loftier range, its snowy peaks lost to the horizon, spoke of the immense geological forces that had shaped this land. The rivers themselves, though their channels soon vanished into the boundless plain, presented a stark contrast. The left-hand, or South fork, measured three hundred and seventy-two yards across, its waters transparent, flowing with a rapid yet unruffled surface, its bed a mosaic of smooth, round stones, characteristic of mountain-born streams. The right-hand, or North fork, though narrower at two hundred yards, was deeper, its current a boiling, rolling torrent, its waters thick and turbid, a whitish-brown hue that had, throughout its long journey, been the unmistakable signature of the Missouri. This turbid character, Lewis reasoned, was the very essence of the Missouri, a testament to its long passage through the open plains, gathering the rich earth in its embrace. The Indians, with their intimate knowledge of this land, had spoken of a river that “scolds at all others,” a description that now resonated with the roiling waters of the North fork. The decision, though not yet formally declared, was taking shape, guided by the immutable laws of nature and the keen observations of these intrepid explorers.
